items.title: Classic parkinsonian triad
items.contentHtml:
Classic triad

  • The classic triad includes tremor, rigidity and bradykinesia; bradykinesia is the most disabling symptom and the one that best responds to levodopa.
  • The rigidity manifests itself as an increase in tone throughout the range of movement, unlike spasticity (depending on speed).
  • The tremor at rest is 4-6 Hz and disappears with voluntary action; bradykinesia is evaluated with repetitive movements (for example, finger tapping).
  • Key fact: the asymmetry of onset is typical of idiopathic Parkinson's disease vs. atypical parkinsonism.

items.title: Tremor in rolling pills
items.contentHtml:
'Pill-rolling' rest tremor

  • The 'pill-rolling' rest tremor is a 4-6 Hz tremor that typically affects the thumb and index finger, simulating the gesture of rolling a pill.
  • It is asymmetrical at onset and disappears with voluntary action or during sleep; reappears with mental stress or distraction.
  • Cheating exam: essential tremor is action/postural and is usually bilateral and symmetrical, without 'pill rolling' rest tremor.
  • The presence of pure rest tremor has high specificity (>90%) for idiopathic Parkinson's disease.

items.title: Stiffness in gear wheel
items.contentHtml:
Cogwheel rigidity

  • Cogwheel rigidity is an increase in muscle tone with a “clicking” or jerking pattern, typically detected in passive flexion-extension of the forearm.
  • It is differentiated from lead pipe rigidity (continuous resistance) by the presence of choppy movements; both are forms of extrapyramidal hypertonia.
  • Pathophysiology: cogwheel rigidity is due to overactivity of stretch reflexes due to lack of dopaminergic inhibition.
  • Key fact: the contralateral activation maneuver (asking the patient to move the other arm) exacerbates cogwheel rigidity.

items.foco: Cause: loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra
items.title: Neuronal loss in substantia nigra
items.contentHtml:
Loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra

  • The main cause is the loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, with a decrease in dopamine in the striatum (putamen and caudate).
  • A loss of 50-60% of neurons is required for motor symptoms to appear; the dopamine deficit in the putamen is more relevant to bradykinesia.
  • Physiopathology: the loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra causes disinhibition of the indirect pathway of the basal ganglia, increasing thalamic inhibition.
  • Trap: in atypical parkinsonism (for example progressive supranuclear palsy) the neuronal loss is more extensive and includes other nuclei.

items.foco: Lewy bodies as a pathological finding
items.title: Lewy bodies
items.contentHtml:
Lewy bodies

  • Lewy bodies are eosinophilic intracytoplasmic inclusions composed of alpha-synuclein and ubiquitin, a distinctive pathological finding.
  • They are located in surviving neurons of the substantia nigra, locus coeruleus and dorsal nucleus of the vagus; Its distribution follows the Braak classification (stages 1-6).
  • Key fact: Lewy bodies are not pathognomonic of Parkinson's disease; They are also seen in dementia with Lewy bodies and multiple system atrophy.
  • The absence of Lewy bodies at necropsy suggests atypical or secondary parkinsonism.

items.foco: Response to levodopa as a diagnostic criterion
items.title: Response to levodopa
items.contentHtml:
Response to levodopa

  • The response to levodopa as a diagnostic criterion is essential: significant improvement (≥30% in UPDRS) after doses of 200-300 mg/day, especially in bradykinesia and rigidity.
  • The response to levodopa as a diagnostic criterion has high sensitivity (>90%) for Parkinson's disease idiopathic; the lack of response suggests atypical parkinsonism.
  • Trick test: in early stages the response may be partial, but the persistence of a good response for >5 years supports the diagnosis.
  • The response is evaluated with the UPDRS (Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale) part III, before and after the dose.

items.foco: Motor fluctuations and dyskinesias due to levodopa
items.title: Fluctuations and dyskinesias
items.contentHtml:
Motor fluctuations and dyskinesias due to levodopa

  • Motor fluctuations and dyskinesias due to levodopa appear after 3-5 years of treatment: wear-off phenomenon, on-off and peak dose dyskinesias.
  • Dyskinesias are involuntary choreiform movements, more frequent in the face, neck and extremities; are related to the dose and duration of treatment.
  • Management: for wearing-off the levodopa dose is fractionated, entacapone (COMT inhibitor) is added or prolonged-release levodopa is used.
  • Key fact: peak dose dyskinesias improve by reducing the dose of levodopa or adding amantadine.

items.title: Non-motor symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Non-motor symptoms

  • The non-motor symptoms: hyposmia, depression, constipation usually precede the motor symptoms by years (prodromal phase).
  • Hyposmia is an early marker (sensitivity ~80%), evaluated with the Sniffin' Sticks test; depression affects 40-50% of patients.
  • Constipation is due to autonomic dysfunction and is correlated with the presence of Lewy bodies in the myenteric plexus.
  • Trap: dementia appears late (in >80% after 10-15 years), but if it is early (<1 year) it suggests dementia with bodies of Lewy.

items.title: Differential diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with atypical parkinsonism

  • The differential diagnosis with atypical parkinsonism includes progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP), multiple system atrophy (MSA), corticobasal degeneration, and dementia with Lewy bodies.
  • PSP: early falls, supranuclear vertical gaze palsy, axial rigidity > appendicular, poor response to levodopa.
  • MSA: severe autonomic failure (orthostatic hypotension, erectile dysfunction), cerebellar ataxia, pyramidal signs.
  • Key fact: the absence of rest tremor in 'pill rolling' and the poor response to levodopa point to parkinsonism atypical.

items.foco: Initial treatment: levodopa/carbidopa or dopamine agonists
items.title: Initial treatment
items.contentHtml:
Initial treatment

  • The initial treatment: levodopa/carbidopa or dopamine agonists is chosen according to age and comorbidities; in patients <60-70 years old, agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) are preferred to delay dyskinesias.
  • In patients >70 years old or with cognitive impairment, levodopa/carbidopa is first line due to better efficacy and lower risk of psychiatric effects.
  • Typical dose: levodopa/carbidopa 100/25 mg three times a day; agonists are titrated slowly (for example pramipexole 0.125 mg three times daily, increase every 5-7 days).
  • Trap: dopamine agonists can cause impulse control disorders (gambling, hypersexuality); levodopa/carbidopa does not.

items.title: Avoid dopamine antagonists
items.contentHtml:
Avoid dopaminergic antagonists

  • It is critical to avoid dopaminergic antagonists (neuroleptics) in patients with Parkinson's disease, as they block D2 receptors and exacerbate parkinsonism.
  • Typical neuroleptics (haloperidol, chlorpromazine) are high risk; atypicals (risperidone, olanzapine) may also worsen motor symptoms.
  • Exception: clozapine and quetiapine are the only antipsychotics that can be used relatively safely (under D2 blockade).
  • Examination trap: metoclopramide and prochlorperazine are also D2 antagonists and should be avoided; Alternatives such as domperidone (does not cross BBB) are safe.

items.foco: Hoehn and Yahr scale for staging
items.title: Hoehn and Yahr scale
items.contentHtml:
Hoehn and Yahr Scale

  • The Hoehn and Yahr Staging Scale classifies Parkinson's disease into 5 stages: 1 (unilateral), 2 (bilateral without balance alteration), 3 (mild postural instability), 4 (severe disability but can walk), 5 (wheelchair or bedridden).
  • It is used to evaluate global progression, but has low sensitivity to subtle changes; the UPDRS is more detailed for follow-up.
  • Key fact: the passage from stage 2 to 3 (appearance of postural instability) marks an important functional milestone and usually occurs after 5-7 years of disease.
  • Trap: the Hoehn and Yahr staging scale does not evaluate non-motor symptoms or dyskinesias.
